context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the city case study data table for Urban Studies 11.024x. I picked a city, but I’m not totally sure how to find and organize the geographic and demographic data they want. Can you help me figure out what categories I should fill in first?
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Before we dive in — have you had a chance to re-read the assignment description carefully? It actually lists the required data categories pretty explicitly. What does it say you need to gather?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting the assignment and asking for orientation. Rather than listing the categories for them, I should point them back to the assignment itself, which already specifies the required data categories. Using the Socratic method, I'll prompt them to look at what the assignment already tells them, so they develop the habit of reading the brief carefully before seeking help.

exchanges.student: I did read it, but I think I’m mixing some of the categories together. I remember location, population, area, income per capita, GDP, race/ethnicity breakdown, and Koppen climate zone — I might be missing one or wording one wrong. Can you help me make sure I’ve got the required list right?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid recall — you're pretty close! Rather than me confirming each item, why don't you put your list side by side with the assignment text one more time? Once you're confident in your categories, what city did you pick, and have you started looking for any of this data yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually recalled the categories quite accurately — they match what the assignment specifies. I should acknowledge this positively without just confirming or correcting in a way that does the thinking for them. I can prompt them to compare their list against the assignment text themselves, and then nudge them toward the next step: actually starting to collect data for their chosen city.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta. I haven’t really started yet because I got stuck on where to find reliable numbers, especially for race/ethnicity and GDP. Can you give me a hint about the best kinds of sources to check first?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to be thinking about. What kinds of organizations or institutions do you think would be responsible for collecting official population and economic data for a country like Indonesia?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuine research challenge — finding reliable sources. Rather than listing sources for them, I should use guided discovery to help them think about what kinds of institutions or organizations typically produce this kind of official demographic and economic data. One or two prompting questions should be enough to get them moving.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing the national statistics office first, maybe the city government too? And maybe World Bank data for GDP? I’m less sure about the race/ethnicity part, because I don’t know if that’s tracked the same way everywhere.
exchanges.tutor: Your source instincts are good. On the race/ethnicity point — you're onto something important. Why do you think demographic categories might differ from country to country, and what might that mean for how you present that data in your table?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is thinking well and has identified strong source types. Their uncertainty about race/ethnicity data is actually a really insightful observation worth affirming and pushing further, since it touches on important conceptual territory relevant to urban studies (how demographic categories differ across national contexts). I should affirm their source instincts briefly and then push them to think harder about the race/ethnicity question, which will enrich their data table.

exchanges.student: I think different countries classify people differently, and some might not collect race data the way the assignment words it. So maybe for Jakarta I should use whatever official demographic categories are actually available and note that they aren’t directly the same as U.S.-style race categories?
exchanges.tutor: That's excellent thinking — noting the limits and context of your data is exactly what good research practice looks like. Now that you have a sense of sources and how to handle tricky categories, it sounds like you're ready to start filling in the table. What's the first data point you're going to look up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong methodological insight on their own — that data categories vary across national contexts and should be presented with appropriate caveats. I should affirm this clearly, as it reflects good academic thinking. Then I should nudge them to move forward and actually start populating their table, since they've been in the planning/thinking phase long enough.

exchanges.student: Probably basic location and population first, since those seem easiest. I’m still a little unsure whether I should use city-proper Jakarta, the special capital region, or the larger metro area if different sources use different boundaries.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to flag that — boundary definitions really do matter. Think about what this data is ultimately for: understanding urban conditions and climate vulnerabilities. Which geographic unit do you think best captures the city as a functioning urban entity for that purpose?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a genuinely important methodological question about geographic boundaries, which is relevant to urban studies. Rather than telling them which boundary to use, I should prompt them to think about what the assignment is asking them to do with this data — understanding urban conditions and climate vulnerabilities — and let them reason through which boundary makes most sense for that purpose. I should also nudge them to be consistent and transparent in their choice.

exchanges.student: I’m torn. My first thought is the larger metro area because that feels more like the real urban system, but I think the assignment probably wants one clearly defined city unit, so maybe the Special Capital Region is safer if I can keep it consistent across all categories. Is that a reasonable choice?
exchanges.tutor: That reasoning is sound — consistency and clarity are key. One thing worth noting: whatever unit you choose, make sure you state it explicitly in your table. Now, let's move forward — have you found any actual numbers yet for location or population?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned through the boundary question well and landed on a defensible, practical answer on their own. I should affirm this briefly without over-validating, and push them to actually start collecting data rather than continuing to deliberate. We're about halfway through the planned conversation length and they haven't started filling anything in yet.

exchanges.student: Not yet, honestly. I got a bit stuck deciding the boundary and then didn’t want to pull mismatched numbers. If I use the Special Capital Region, should I try to get population, area, GDP, and income all for that same unit even if metro-area figures are easier to find?
exchanges.tutor: You've already answered this yourself — yes, stay consistent with your chosen unit, and note any exceptions. We've spent a lot of time on this question. Time to actually open a browser and find some numbers. What does BPS (Indonesia's national statistics office) say about the Special Capital Region's population?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles on the boundary question — this is the third or fourth exchange touching on the same concern. They've already reasoned to a good answer. I should be direct about the redundancy, briefly affirm their conclusion, and firmly redirect them to actually start collecting data. Being less Socratic here is appropriate given the pattern.

exchanges.student: Yeah, fair point. I haven’t looked it up yet, so I can’t answer that directly — can you give me a quick hint for what page or search terms I should use on BPS so I can find the DKI Jakarta population fast?
exchanges.tutor: That's for you to figure out — navigating sources is part of the research skill this assignment is building. Try the BPS website directly and see what you can find. Come back when you have a number and we can talk about what to do with it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do their research for them by providing specific search terms or page navigation. That crosses into assistant territory. I should decline and remind them that searching is their job, while keeping it brief and redirecting them to take action.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try that. If I find multiple population numbers from different years, I’ll bring back the one I think fits best and check it with you.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. When you bring back a number, make sure you also note the source and the year — that context matters for a data table. Go look it up!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is in a good place — they're about to do the actual research. I should give a brief, encouraging send-off that also reminds them to note the year of their data, which is important for a data table. No need for a question here; the ball is in their court.
